abstract: This paper proposes a quantitative method for detecting and measuring 'information voids' — periods when reliable content fails to meet public demand for information on a topic — by leveraging longitudinal data from social media and web searches. The authors operationalize voids as anomalous imbalances between information supply (Facebook, Twitter, GDELT news) and demand (Wikipedia page-views, Google Trends), constructing an 'information delta' time series and applying STL decomposition with IQR-based anomaly detection. Applied to COVID-19 vaccine discourse across six European countries (January 2020–April 2021), the method identifies persistent voids around key vaccine-related events and shows that these voids correlate with reduced circulation of high-credibility content and increased prevalence of misinformation.
key_claims: Information voids can be operationalized and quantitatively detected as statistically anomalous negative imbalances between information demand and supply in the online ecosystem.
The online information ecosystem can be classified into five regimes — Void, Lack, Balance, Abundance, Overabundance — based on the magnitude of demand–supply imbalance.
Information voids tend to persist longer than periods of overabundance, indicating asymmetric recovery dynamics in the information ecosystem.

Information voids are empirically associated with a lower share of highly credible content and a higher prevalence of misinformation, supporting their inclusion in mechanistic explanations of misinformation emergence.
Major external events (vaccine roll-outs, EMA authorizations, AstraZeneca suspension) trigger immediate and measurable disruptions in the demand–supply balance.
methods: Data science pipeline combining time-series rescaling by expected value, computation of an 'information delta' (rescaled supply minus rescaled demand), STL (Seasonal–Trend decomposition using Loess), and IQR-based anomaly detection on the remainder component.

Supply proxies: Facebook posts (via CrowdTangle), Twitter posts (v2 full-archive academic API), and online news articles (GDELT Summary database); demand proxies: Wikipedia page-views and Google Trends.

Keyword-based data collection across five COVID-19 vaccine brands (AstraZeneca, Moderna, Pfizer, Johnson & Johnson, Sputnik V) for six countries (Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, Spain, UK), January 2020–April 2021.
Validation of the pipeline on synthetic Gaussian time series with injected anomalies (1σ to 15σ), measuring precision and F1 score.
Integration with NewsGuard credibility scores (0–100) to assess content quality across non-anomaly, void, and overabundance periods on Facebook and Twitter.
Case study analyses around EMA Moderna authorization and AstraZeneca suspension/reinstatement events.
findings: On synthetic data, the method achieves mean precision >90% for anomaly magnitudes above 6σ and mean F1 score >0.68 above 9σ.
Empirically, the percentage of anomalous days ranged from ~7.9% to ~9.9% across countries and platforms, with notable differences in the balance of positive vs. negative anomalies by country.
Information voids reached durations of up to 29 consecutive days (e.g., Twitter for Sputnik V in Germany; Facebook for Moderna in Italy), generally outlasting overabundance episodes.

Cumulative anomalies rose sharply around the Moderna EMA authorization (e.g., Facebook anomalies in Italy rising from 30% to 81% within a month) and around the AstraZeneca suspension (e.g., 35% to 88% in Italy).

During voids, highly credible content (NewsGuard score = 100) dropped to 20.84% on Facebook and 23.87% on Twitter (versus 33.68% and 31.62% in non-anomalous periods), while content from highly unreliable sources rose to 6.1% on Facebook and 27.28% on Twitter.
Cross-correlations between demand and supply series peaked at lag 0, indicating largely synchronous responsiveness of the ecosystem.
contributions: Introduces the first systematic, replicable quantitative pipeline for operationalizing and detecting information voids using longitudinal multi-platform data.
Proposes a five-regime taxonomy (Void, Lack, Balance, Abundance, Overabundance) of information ecosystem states grounded in supply–demand dynamics.
Provides empirical evidence linking information voids to degraded information quality and elevated misinformation prevalence, motivating their inclusion in mechanistic models of misinformation.

Offers a domain-agnostic, reproducible methodology applicable to public health, politics, environmental issues, and emerging crises, with implications for infodemic management and risk communication preparedness.
Validates the approach on both synthetic data and a real-world case study of COVID-19 vaccine discourse across six European countries.
framing: The paper positions itself within the literature on the attention economy, misinformation diffusion on social media, and infodemic management, building on prior work conceptualizing the online information environment as a market shaped by supply and demand. It explicitly addresses the gap identified in recent discussions (Golebiewski & boyd; Purnat et al.; Nature editorials) about the absence of operational definitions and measurement tools for 'data voids' or 'information voids', moving the concept from qualitative assessment toward quantitative, mechanistic analysis.
